title: Vashon High School

infobox.value: [St. Louis Public Schools](/wiki/St._Louis_Public_Schools "St. Louis Public Schools")

is a [high school](/wiki/High_school "High school") of the [St. Louis Public Schools](/wiki/Public_school_system "Public school system") in [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"). When it opened in 1927, it was the second high school for black students in St. Louis.[[1]](#cite_note-bosenbecker_119-120-1)

Comprehensive public high school in the United States

**Vashon High School** is a [high school](/wiki/High_school "High school") of the [St. Louis Public Schools](/wiki/Public_school_system "Public school system") in [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"). When it opened in 1927, it was the second high school for black students in St. Louis.[[1]](#cite_note-bosenbecker_119-120-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vashon_High_School&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Designed by Rockwell M. Milligan, the school opened on September 11, 1927, and it was named in honor of two [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") educators: [George Boyer Vashon](/wiki/George_Boyer_Vashon "George Boyer Vashon"), the first black graduate of [Oberlin College](/wiki/Oberlin_College "Oberlin College"), and his son, John Boyer Vashon.[[1]](#cite_note-bosenbecker_119-120-1) Located at 3026 Laclede Avenue, the school was built for slightly less than $1.2 million[[1]](#cite_note-bosenbecker_119-120-1) ($22,241,379 today[[4]](#cite_note-inflation-US-4)). Vashon was the second high school built for black students in the St. Louis Public Schools, after [Sumner High School](/wiki/Sumner_High_School_(St._Louis,_Missouri) "Sumner High School (St. Louis, Missouri)").[[5]](#cite_note-stlpd02181990-5)[[6]](#cite_note-dillon_236-7-6)

Four members of the Vashon glee club created the popular singing group [The Four Vagabonds](/wiki/The_Four_Vagabonds "The Four Vagabonds") in 1933.[[7]](#cite_note-7) From 1935 to 1949, Vashon's boys basketball program won six state titles as part of the Missouri Negro Interscholastic Athletic Association.[[8]](#cite_note-stlpd03122001-8) Vashon was barred from joining the Missouri State High School Activities Association until 1949, and between 1949 and 1954, it was prohibited from participating in both MNIAA tournaments and MSHSAA state tournaments.[[8]](#cite_note-stlpd03122001-8)

In June 1963, the school moved to the Hadley Vocational-Technical High School building at 3405 Bell Avenue, and the original building became part of [Harris–Stowe State University](/wiki/Harris%E2%80%93Stowe_State_University "Harris–Stowe State University").[[1]](#cite_note-bosenbecker_119-120-1)[[5]](#cite_note-stlpd02181990-5) The Bell Avenue building had been built in the early 1930s with large shop classrooms that were subsequently divided into classrooms and offices with partition walls, causing noise problems throughout the school.[[5]](#cite_note-stlpd02181990-5) Its architectural design also strongly resembled a factory, and according to a local newspaper report, "the main school building, gym and auditorium make one think the people inside might be manufacturing cars or widgets."[[5]](#cite_note-stlpd02181990-5) The move was accompanied by protests in the local community and a student march against the transfer.[[9]](#cite_note-stlpd03092001-9)

After the transfer, Vashon students were offered more vocational classes, including auto repair, fashion design, cosmetology, dry cleaning, woodworking, shoe repair, drafting, and commercial cooking.[[6]](#cite_note-dillon_236-7-6) From 1974 to 2006, Vashon's boys basketball team was coached by Floyd Irons, a Vashon alumnus who became one of the winningest basketball coaches in Missouri history.[[10]](#cite_note-stlpd07132006-10) Irons coached the team to four state championships in the 1980s.[[11]](#cite_note-stlpd03032004-11)

In 1990, the Board of Education considered several options to deal with noise problems and facilities issues at Vashon; among the options were closure of Vashon, partial renovation, full renovation, or complete demolition and replacement.[[5]](#cite_note-stlpd02181990-5) Ultimately the Board decided against closure and opted for partial renovation of the building; support from the school's alumni and the school's strong boys basketball program played a role in the decision to keep the school open.[[9]](#cite_note-stlpd03092001-9) In 1994, the Vashon boys basketball team won another state championship under Irons.[[11]](#cite_note-stlpd03032004-11)

In August 2002, Vashon moved again, to a new building at 3035 Cass Avenue designed by Kennedy and Associates and built at a cost of $47.3  million.[[1]](#cite_note-bosenbecker_119-120-1) The boys' basketball team won five state championships in the 2000s: in 2000, 2001, 2002, 2004, and 2006. In 2005, the school's boys' basketball program was ranked as the top program in the United States by [USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today").[[12]](#cite_note-stlpd01262005-12)

In 2006, the *[Riverfront Times](/wiki/Riverfront_Times "Riverfront Times")*, a local newspaper, published an investigative report that detailed extensive allegations of misconduct by Floyd Irons as coach at Vashon.[[13]](#cite_note-rft11012006-13) The allegations eventually led the Missouri State High School Activities Association (MSHSAA) to strip Vashon of its 2001, 2002, and 2006 titles due to violations of MSHSAA rules on recruiting and eligibility.[[14]](#cite_note-rft06182008-14) In July 2006, Irons was dismissed as coach and administrator at Vashon, and he was replaced as head coach by [Anthony Bonner](/wiki/Anthony_Bonner "Anthony Bonner"), a retired [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA") player and Vashon alumnus.[[15]](#cite_note-rft02112009-15) Bonner himself resigned in 2009.[[15]](#cite_note-rft02112009-15)

## Sports and activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vashon_High_School&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sports and activities")]

Since 1934, the school has won 14 state basketball championships – six as a member of the Missouri Negro Interscholastic Athletic Association and then eight as a member of the [Missouri State High School Activities Association](/wiki/Missouri_State_High_School_Activities_Association "Missouri State High School Activities Association").[[16]](#cite_note-mshsaachamps-16)
For the 2011–2012 school year, the school offered 18 activities approved by the [Missouri State High School Activities Association](/wiki/Missouri_State_High_School_Activities_Association "Missouri State High School Activities Association") (MSHSAA): baseball, boys and girls basketball, cheerleading, boys and girls cross country, football, music activities, boys and girls soccer, softball, speech and debate, boys and girls tennis, boys and girls track and field, girls volleyball, and wrestling.[[17]](#cite_note-mshsaaevents-17) In addition to its current activities, its students have won several state championships:[[8]](#cite_note-stlpd03122001-8)[[18]](#cite_note-mshsaachamps2-18)

* Boys basketball: 1935, 1936, 1944, 1947, 1948, 1949, 1971, 1983, 1985, 1986, 1988, 1994, 2000, 2004, 2016, 2017[[19]](#cite_note-bballnote-19)[[20]](#cite_note-bballnote2-20)
* Boys cross country: 1956, 1958, 1960
* Boys track and field: 1984

The school also has produced one individual wrestling state champion.[[18]](#cite_note-mshsaachamps2-18)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vashon_High_School&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable people")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Henry_Armstrong.jpg/250px-Henry_Armstrong.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_Armstrong.jpg)

Alumnus [Henry Armstrong](/wiki/Henry_Armstrong "Henry Armstrong") became a world champion boxer.

### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vashon_High_School&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Faculty")]

* [Anthony Bonner](/wiki/Anthony_Bonner "Anthony Bonner") (born 1968), professional basketball player[[21]](#cite_note-stlpd08181992-21)
* [Frank Lunsford Williams](/wiki/Frank_Lunsford_Williams "Frank Lunsford Williams") (1864–1953) principal from 1932 to 1940[[22]](#cite_note-NKAA-22)

### Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vashon_High_School&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Alumni")]

* [Virgil Akins](/wiki/Virgil_Akins "Virgil Akins"), world champion boxer[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [Devon Alexander](/wiki/Devon_Alexander "Devon Alexander"), world champion boxer[[24]](#cite_note-stlpd021720042-24)
* [Henry Armstrong](/wiki/Henry_Armstrong "Henry Armstrong"), world champion boxer
* Bob Beeks, NFL official (1968–91), first African-American official to work in five Super Bowls
* [Anthony Bonner](/wiki/Anthony_Bonner "Anthony Bonner"), professional basketball player (later became faculty and basketball coach at Vashon)[[25]](#cite_note-rft021120092-25)
* [Jerry Jerome Brown Jr](/wiki/Jerry_Brown_(American_football) "Jerry Brown (American football)"), NFL and CFL football player; last played with the Dallas Cowboys.
* [Butler By'not'e](/wiki/Butler_By%27not%27e "Butler By'not'e"), professional football player[[26]](#cite_note-stlpd062119932-26)
* [Jason Chorak](/wiki/Jason_Chorak "Jason Chorak"), former professional football player[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* [Mac Cody](/wiki/Mac_Cody "Mac Cody"), professional football player[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* [Chick Finney](/wiki/Chick_Finney "Chick Finney"), jazz pianist
* [Will Franklin](/wiki/Will_Franklin_(American_football) "Will Franklin (American football)"), professional football player[[29]](#cite_note-stlpd042820082-29)
* [Lloyd L. Gaines](/wiki/Lloyd_L._Gaines "Lloyd L. Gaines"), key player in *[Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada](/wiki/Missouri_ex_rel._Gaines_v._Canada "Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada")*, which desegregated the [University of Missouri School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Missouri_School_of_Law "University of Missouri School of Law")[[30]](#cite_note-dillon_236-72-30)
* [Vivian Gibson](/wiki/Vivian_Gibson "Vivian Gibson"), Author of "The Last Children of Mill Creek" documenting her childhood growing up in Mill Creek Valley, a segregated community in the heart of St. Louis that was destroyed by Urban Renewal.[[31]](#cite_note-31)===Alumni===
* [Grant Green](/wiki/Grant_Green "Grant Green"), jazz guitarist[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* [Donny Hathaway](/wiki/Donny_Hathaway "Donny Hathaway"), singer and songwriter
* [Eddie Hopson](/wiki/Eddie_Hopson "Eddie Hopson"), Olympic boxer (class of 1990) and IBF Super Feather weight Champion (1995)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Elston Howard](/wiki/Elston_Howard "Elston Howard"), professional baseball player, first African-American player for the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")[[30]](#cite_note-dillon_236-72-30)
* [Oliver Lee Jackson](/wiki/Oliver_Lee_Jackson "Oliver Lee Jackson"), painter, sculptor, printmaker, and educator.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-:32-34)
* [Terry Kennedy](/wiki/Terry_Kennedy_(politician) "Terry Kennedy (politician)"), politician, former activist, and journalist
* [Roscoe L. Koontz](/wiki/Roscoe_L._Koontz "Roscoe L. Koontz"), pioneer in [health physics](/wiki/Health_physics "Health physics")[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Jimmy McKinney](/wiki/Jimmy_McKinney "Jimmy McKinney"), professional basketball player[[36]](#cite_note-stlpd122020022-36)
* [Theodore McMillian](/wiki/Theodore_McMillian "Theodore McMillian"), judge of the Missouri Court of Appeals and United States Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit; first African-American judge on either court[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* [Leon Spinks](/wiki/Leon_Spinks "Leon Spinks"), U.S. Olympic and professional boxer best known for beating [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali")
* [Michael Spinks](/wiki/Michael_Spinks "Michael Spinks"), champion Olympic and professional boxer[[38]](#cite_note-nndb2-38)
* [Norris Stevenson](/wiki/Norris_Stevenson "Norris Stevenson"), first African-American scholarship athlete for the [University of Missouri](/wiki/University_of_Missouri "University of Missouri") football program[[39]](#cite_note-stltoday2-39)
* [Clark Terry](/wiki/Clark_Terry "Clark Terry"), jazz musician
* [Morris Towns](/wiki/Morris_Towns "Morris Towns"), professional football player[[40]](#cite_note-stlpd051519912-40)
* [Quincy Troupe](/wiki/Quincy_Troupe "Quincy Troupe"), author and poet (attended Vashon, but transferred to Beaumont)[[41]](#cite_note-thefreelibrary2-41)
* [Quincy Trouppe](/wiki/Quincy_Trouppe "Quincy Trouppe"), former [MLB](/wiki/MLB "MLB") player ([Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians"))
* [Maxine Waters](/wiki/Maxine_Waters "Maxine Waters"), member of the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives")

["Anthony Bonner Bids Adieu to Vashon"](http://blogs.riverfronttimes.com/dailyrft/2009/02/anthony_bonner_bids_adieu_to_vashon_high_school_heading_to_new_york_knicks.php). *Riverfront Times*. Retrieved January 15, 2012.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-mshsaachamps_16-0)** Prior to its integration in 1954, the Missouri State High School Activities Association (MSHSAA) did not permit black schools to participate in postseason tournaments, and Vashon competed in the Missouri Negro Interscholastic Athletic Association (MNIAA), a state athletic association for black schools. For MSHSAA championship counts for Missouri high schools, see
    Missouri State High School Activities Association (2007). [State Championship Histories by Sport](https://web.archive.org/web/20120223165850/http://www.mshsaa.org/resources/pdf/State%20Championship%20Histories%20by%20Sport.pdf) (PDF) (Report). MSHSAA. pp. 129–133. Archived from [the original](http://www.mshsaa.org/resources/pdf/State%20Championship%20Histories%20by%20Sport.pdf) (PDF) on February 23, 2012. Retrieved January 15, 2012.; for MNIAA championship information, see John Duxbury (March 12, 2001). "Vashon Can Claim 14 Titles, With Six Before Integration – School Was Not Allowed to Play in MSHSAA Tournament Until 1954". *St. Louis Post-Dispatch*. Vashon had previously held the state championships for 2001, 2004, and 2006; however, its titles were removed due to violations of MSHSAA rules, leaving the team with 8 MSHSAA championships (1971, 1983, 1985, 1986, 1988, 1994, 2000, 2002) and 6 MNIAA championships (1935, 1936, 1944, 1947, 1948, 1949). For information on the violations, see Kristen Hinman (June 18, 2008). ["Basketball by the Book, Part 6: Fouled Out"](http://www.riverfronttimes.com/2008-06-18/news/fouled-out-vashon-high-is-stripped-of-three-missouri-state-basketball-titles/). *Riverfront Times*. Retrieved January 15, 2012.
